segments.title: Which Coreopsis is under discussion?
segments.summary: The opening prevents a common identity mix-up by separating HMS Coreopsis from a similarly named drifter involved in a submarine sinking off Scotland.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins by warning that HMS Coreopsis should not be confused with a similarly named naval drifter. The other vessel took part with a destroyer in sinking a submarine off the Scottish coast. That distinction matters because the rest of the video follows the Flower-class sloop and its unusually varied career. The brief correction is a useful guide through the repeated small-ship names found in Royal Navy records.

evidence.note: The transcript distinguishes HMS Coreopsis from a similarly named drifter associated with a destroyer-led submarine sinking off Scotland.

segments.title: Coreopsis as a concealed-armament Q-ship
segments.summary: Coreopsis served as a decoy warship with concealed guns, operating with the Grand Fleet, in the Mediterranean, and from Gibraltar under false names.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents Coreopsis as an outstanding example of the First World War Q-ship. Concealed armament allowed the vessel to appear harmless before engaging an enemy that approached. Her assignments ranged from the Grand Fleet and Mediterranean service to operations from Gibraltar under the false names Bursley and Biggo. She was sold for breaking up in 1922, closing a career that also helped shape the later conception of the Royal Navy sloop.

evidence.note: The transcript describes concealed-armament decoy service, Grand Fleet and Mediterranean deployments, Gibraltar false names, and sale in 1922.

segments.title: A compact hull with useful carrying margin
segments.summary: Coreopsis's loaded tonnage, displacement, dimensions, and draft show how a small sloop still carried a meaningful margin of stores.
segments.body: The video contrasts fully laden tonnage with displacement, highlighting roughly 450 tons available for fuel, stores, armament, and other loads. Dr. Clarke then sets out a hull a little over 262 feet long with a 35-foot beam. Fully laden draft could reach 13 feet 8 inches. Together, those figures explain the compact but useful carrying capacity behind the ship's multiple roles.

evidence.note: The transcript gives loaded and displacement tonnage, carrying difference, length, beam, and draft.

segments.title: Armament and endurance for a heavily armed small ship
segments.summary: Sixteen knots, coal endurance, four-inch and lighter guns, depth charges, and a crew of 92 reveal a small vessel built for demanding service.
segments.body: Coreopsis combined a top speed of 16 knots with capacity for 260 tons of coal. Her stated armament included two quick-firing four-inch Mark IV guns, two 12-pounders, depth charges, and sometimes three-pounders. Dr. Clarke consequently describes the type as a heavily armed little ship and gives a complement of 92. The inventory shows how much fighting power and manpower the design packed into a small hull.

evidence.note: The transcript lists speed, coal, gun and depth-charge armament, and the complement.

segments.title: From Flower-class vessel to modern sloop concept
segments.summary: Coreopsis helps explain the First World War roots of the later Royal Navy sloop, even though the class was originally ordered for minesweeping rather than Q-ship duty.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses Coreopsis to identify a formative stage in the development of the Royal Navy sloop. The Flower-class vessel could be employed as a Q-ship, but that was an adaptation rather than its original purpose. The class had first been ordered for minesweeping work. This combination of an initial specialist requirement and later operational flexibility helps explain the sloop's interwar development.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the vessel an origin point for later sloops, notes Q-ship employment, and says the type was first ordered for minesweeping.

segments.title: Why peacetime small ships needed several roles
segments.summary: The overlap between sloops and minesweepers reflects both operational utility and the politics of winning funds for small warships between wars.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes the sloop and minesweeper as two sides of the same coin during the 1910s, 1920s, and 1930s. A small warship needed to be capable of several kinds of work rather than tied to a single narrow task. Wartime emergency construction could attract money quickly, but peacetime programmes required a stronger political justification. Minesweeping supplied a clear requirement while a multi-purpose hull gave the navy value across a wider range of duties.

evidence.note: The transcript connects sloop-minesweeper overlap, multi-purpose capability, wartime funding, and the need to justify peacetime construction.
